title: :where()
description: Works like :is() — matches any selector in its list — but always contributes zero specificity. Base styles written with :where() can be overridden by any class selector, making them safe as defaults.
html:
Default heading

Highlighted — should be cyan

Another default heading

Also highlighted — should be cyan
challenge: The highlighted headings should be cyan but there are no base heading styles yet. Add :where(.prose) h2 { color: #a855f7; } as the base rule — its zero specificity means .highlight (a plain class selector) can still override it.
realWorldUsage: Design system reset layers — base typographic styles, spacing resets, and form defaults are written with :where() so any component class can override them without needing higher specificity or !important. It is the CSS equivalent of Sass's !default: a value that applies unless something else is present.
